reviews.review_description:
Smasher is a solid cyberpunk arcade shooter that’s fun from the very first minutes. The tutorial is particularly well designed, introducing mechanics gradually across multiple levels without ever overwhelming the player. You’re always learning something new, but in a natural and enjoyable way.

The level variety really stands out: hacking terminals while fending off enemy waves, dodging projectiles from massive end-of-level bosses, stopping enemies before they reach emergency buttons, and shooting drones attacking from all angles. The game avoids feeling linear, and the story unfolds smoothly as you progress.

One of the most refreshing mechanics is the ability to grab in-game objects and throw them, adding fluidity and creativity to combat and reinforcing the arcade feel. Everything flows well, keeping the focus on fast, satisfying action rather than complexity for complexity’s sake.

Stylish, dynamic, and well executed, Smasher is a highly recommended arcade shooter for players looking for intense action with smart design and cyberpunk flair.

This game is pure, fast-paced, neon fun from the moment you put the headset on. The gameplay is satisfying, combining quick reactions, physical movement, and that just one more round feeling that keeps pulling you back in. 
I do suggest you start with the tutorial; I didn’t and was a bit lost so restarted, and after playing the tutorial things made a whole lot more sense.  Also I would ask the devs to look at a height adjustment option in the settings, if it’s there I missed it, I play a lot of games seated and could not quite get it set right so ended up playing standing.
That said the VR mechanics are pretty spot-on, with smooth controls and great hand eye tracking that make the action feel natural and immersive.
Whether you’re jumping in for a short burst or a longer session, VR Smasher delivers high-energy entertainment, replay ability, and a good sense of progression.

After years on the VR action market feels like all mechanics have been explored. Smasher pleasantly disrupts this pattern. The disposable weapon mechanic solves two problems at once: removes the reload routine and physically engages the player forcing them to make throws dodges and constantly seek new "toys." It's not just a "shooter" it's a "thrower" in the best sense. The level design is built around the idea of making a fresh gameplay conditions each level: protecting a button, hacking a terminal under fire, minding conveyor belts. This creates a multi-layered challenge requiring not just good aim but also attention management. The comparison to Superhot is apt in terms of purity of the core idea and to Borderlands in the spirit of its crazy arsenal dangerous reloading and atmosphere. For an indie studio this level of ambition is interesting.

A great pick for fans of arcade shooters and frantic VR combat, especially if you enjoy improvisational play, nice not to have to worry about reloading, just grab another gun, as much as i enjoyed playing, this might be more of a short session thrill rather than a deep lasting adventure

When I started playing, it took me some time to understand how to use magic. Then I learned how to use it like a pro. I liked Scrubby’s monologues because they are very funny. The game looks good, and it feels exciting, like being on a treadmill. I think this game is worth the money
Awesome game! Thank you devs! Looking forward to new content and updates.

Haven’t played very far into this game yet but so far is a very fun shooter. Increasing challenges as you go with lots of fun guns and spells to discover for fighting the attacking enemies. Mechanics seem pretty polished already and the visuals are pleasing to look at. I would like it if you had the option for holding the gun in the left hand and the spell stones in the right hand. As a left hander I feel like this would feel even better when playing. Would give it 5 stars otherwise.

The weapons and spells are varied and genuinely fun to use! The core mechanics of dodging, throwing, and shooting all feel solid, which is especially important in a VR title. On a mechanical level, the foundation is definitely there.

However, as a complete experience, the game lacks impact. For a VR wave shooter, I expected more intensity and immersion. The early missions and first boss felt repetitive, mostly just shooting with occasional dodging, without much real strategy or challenge. Even on medium difficulty, I haven’t died once, which makes progression feel less rewarding.

There are also immersion issues. Getting hit doesn’t provide meaningful haptic feedback, and there isn’t a clear or satisfying indication of health. In VR, feedback is crucial, and without it, combat feels less engaging and less physical than it should.

The first boss was underwhelming, though I will say the second boss showed improvement in design, even if it was still relatively easy. That gives me some hope. The game has potential, and with balance tweaks, stronger feedback systems, and more strategic encounter design, it could become something great.

For now, I’m cautiously optimistic and plan to edit this review after future updates.
Fun game.  Cool graphics, smooth play.  Great tutorial to start.  Lots of action, weapons, spells. Just a lot of fun to play.

Bosses really shine, the best part of the game. Katana is also very satisfying. The gunplay is great, magic is cool... but man, I wish there was a tiny bit more feedback when you hit enemies with throwing. Sometimes in the chaos it's hard to tell if your throws are registered. Still, had fun.
A great lil shooter with some cool ideas and mechanics..

Honestly, first boss is pretty hard even on medium diff, died a ton at the start (how to dodge the death ray???) but maybe I just suck.
Apart from that game is pretty fun and worth the money
